scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Yesterday in ED majors I assessed a 62-year-old man with pleuritic chest pain. I considered ACS, PE and pneumonia, used Wells score, reviewed ECG and CXR, discussed with my consultant, and arranged CTPA. I learned to state my risk assessment more clearly when escalating.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a 62-year-old with pleuritic chest pain, formulated a differential diagnosis, used clinical decision rules, and discussed the diagnostic strategy (CTPA) with a consultant, making it a classic case-based discussion of clinical reasoning.

The trainee includes a specific personal reflection on their communication style ('learned to state my risk assessment more clearly when escalating'), which warrants a reflective practice entry to document this professional development.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I assessed a 62-year-old man presenting with pleuritic chest pain. My differential diagnoses included acute coronary syndrome (ACS), pulmonary embolism (PE), and pneumonia.

To risk-stratify the patient, I calculated a Wells score, reviewed the electrocardiogram (ECG), and evaluated the chest X-ray (CXR). After synthesising these findings, I discussed the case with the ED consultant and arranged a CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) to rule out a PE.
draft_fields.reflection: This case of structured communication during clinical escalation. I learned that I need to state my risk assessment and scoring outcomes more clearly when discussing management plans with seniors.
key_capabilities: SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

issues: Missing required fields: Clinical Setting

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Today I performed DC cardioversion for unstable atrial fibrillation as a formal DOPS. My consultant observed each step directly. I checked consent, monitoring and sedation plan, delivered a synchronised shock, and reflected on calling for airway support earlier.

recommended_forms.rationale: Directly observed hands-on procedure with senior supervision; DOPS is the specific assessed-procedure form.
The performed procedure can also be recorded in the procedural log.
Feedback/reflection was included alongside the procedural assessment.

draft_fields.trainee_performance: I performed a synchronised DC cardioversion under the direct observation of my consultant for a formal DOPS assessment. Before the procedure, I confirmed patient consent, ensured appropriate continuous monitoring was in place, and established a clear sedation plan. I then safely delivered the synchronised shock.
draft_fields.reflection:
During the procedure, I realized that I should have called for airway support earlier in the planning phase to maximum safety during sedation. 

In future resuscitations requiring sedation or cardioversion, I will airway support is requested and present at an earlier stage before the procedure.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC2: be expert in fluid management and circulatory support in critically ill patients (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.form_name: Direct Observation of Procedural Skills - ACCS
scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. During my ACCS anaesthetic placement I inserted a chest drain using Seldinger technique as an observed DOPS on 5 July 2026. My supervisor watched the procedure. I confirmed landmarks, used sterile technique and reflected on slowing down before dilating.

recommended_forms.rationale: Trainee-performed procedure without a clear direct-assessment signal.

draft_fields.case_observed: Insertion of a chest drain using the Seldinger technique under direct supervision.
draft_fields.reflection: During the procedure, I confirmed the landmarks and maintained a strict sterile technique. I reflected on the importance of slowing down before dilating to safe and controlled tract preparation.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. A consultant directly observed me taking a focused history and examination from a 7-year-old with wheeze in paediatric ED. I explained the plan to the parent and received feedback on structuring my assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee was directly observed by a consultant during a focused history and examination of a paediatric patient, including explaining the plan to the parent, with immediate feedback provided.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning: I took a focused history and performed a clinical examination on a 7-year-old presenting with a wheeze. I then explained our management and treatment plan to the child's parent.
draft_fields.reflection: The consultant directly observed the encounter and provided constructive feedback on how I can better structure my clinical assessment when managing paediatric respiratory presentations. I will apply this feedback to make my future assessments more systematic.
key_capabilities: SLO5 KC1: be expert in assessing and managing all children and young adult patients attending the ED (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. On an evening acute take I managed six patients across majors and resus, including sepsis, chest pain and a frail fall. My consultant observed my prioritisation, handover and escalation decisions over the shift.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a cohort of six acute patients across majors and resus during a shift, with a consultant observing their prioritisation, clinical decision-making, and department management.
The session involved direct consultant observation of non-technical skills (prioritisation, handover, escalation, and team coordination) over a substantial part of an acute shift.

draft_fields.cases_observed: I managed six patients across majors and resuscitation during an evening acute take. The cases included a patient presenting with sepsis, another with chest pain, and a frail elderly patient following a fall.
draft_fields.reflection:
Managing multiple complex patients across different areas of the department simultaneously required active prioritization and clear communication. I had to balance the immediate resuscitation needs of the sepsis patient with the diagnostic workup for the chest pain and the safe assessment of the frail faller.

Having the consultant observe my performance provided reassurance regarding my escalation decisions and the structure of my handovers. I realized the importance of maintaining an overview of the department's flow while directing individual patient care.

In future shifts, I will continue to refine my structured approach to managing a mixed caseload, that I escalate early when resus capacity or patient safety is pressured.
key_capabilities: SLO2 KC2: aware of when it is appropriate to review patients remotely or directly and able to teach these principles to others (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I acted as EPIC for a crowded evening ED shift. I allocated junior doctors, held a safety huddle, escalated capacity concerns to site management and reflected on protecting time for re-review of high-risk patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee acted as EPIC (Emergency Physician in Charge) during a crowded shift, managing junior doctor allocation, leading a safety huddle, and coordinating site-level escalation.
The trainee explicitly reflects on the shift-level experience, specifically looking back at how to protect clinical time for the re-review of high-risk patients during periods of high crowding.

draft_fields.leadership_context: I acted as EPIC (Emergency Physician in Charge) during a crowded evening shift in the Emergency Department. The department was under significant capacity and flow pressures, requiring active coordination of the junior medical team and liaison with wider hospital site management.
draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
During this shift, I took on the operational leadership role to maintain department safety and flow. I coordinated the medical team by allocating junior doctors to appropriate clinical areas based on their experience and the immediate needs of the department.

To shared situational awareness, I held a safety huddle with the nursing and medical teams to highlight high-risk patients and potential bottlenecks. As crowding increased and exit block became a issue, I escalated capacity concerns to the hospital site management team to advocate for inpatient bed allocation and support ED outflow.
draft_fields.reflection:
Managing a crowded department as EPIC highlights the tension between operational flow and clinical safety. I found that active communication through structured huddles helps align the multidisciplinary team and everyone is aware of the most vulnerable patients under our care.

I reflected on the importance of actively protecting my own time to re-review high-risk patients, especially when operational demands and administrative interruptions are high. In future shifts, I will I delegate specific tasks earlier and establish clear boundaries to safeguard clinical oversight of complex patients.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE. I framed a PICO question, searched NICE and a recent meta-analysis, and discussed how I would apply the evidence safely in low-risk patients. I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee formulated a clinical question using the PICO framework, searched the literature (NICE and a meta-analysis), and appraised evidence regarding age-adjusted D-dimer in suspected PE.
The trainee includes a clear personal reflection on what they learned ('I learned to...') and how they will adapt their future clinical documentation and patient communication.

draft_fields.situation: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected pulmonary embolism (PE).
draft_fields.search_methodology: I searched NICE and a recent meta-analysis.
draft_fields.apply_to_practice: Applying the evidence safely in low-risk patients.
draft_fields.communicate_to_patient: I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.
key_capabilities: SLO10 KC1: be able to appraise, synthesise, communicate and use research evidence to develop EM care (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I delivered a 25-minute teaching session to four junior doctors on ECGs in chest pain. A consultant observed the session and gave feedback on pacing and checking understanding.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee delivered a formal, 25-minute scheduled teaching session that was directly observed by a consultant who provided feedback.
An assessor observed the session specifically to evaluate and provide feedback on the trainee's teaching skills, such as pacing and checking learner understanding.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I reviewed feedback from nurses, juniors and consultants. Themes were calm leadership and clear explanations, with a development point around documenting senior discussions more consistently.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee has gathered and reviewed 360-degree feedback from multiple colleagues (nurses, juniors, consultants) regarding their clinical practice and communication.

The trainee is reflecting on multi-source feedback themes (calm leadership, clear explanations) and identifying a professional development point regarding consistent documentation of senior discussions.

draft_fields.context: I reviewed my Multi-Source Feedback (MSF) containing responses from nursing staff, junior colleagues, and consultants during my ST5 placement.
draft_fields.reflection:
The feedback was reassuring, particularly the positive themes around my calm leadership style and providing clear explanations to the team during busy shifts.

As a development point, several respondents noted that I could document my senior discussions more consistently in the patient notes. I will make a conscious effort to clearly record the name and plan of any consultant or senior colleague I discuss cases with to improve safety and communication.

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. Baseline audit was 42 cases, we introduced a triage prompt and re-audit showed improvement from 58 to 39 minutes. I presented it at governance. My PDP summary this year is to improve quality improvement leadership. Next year I will focus on embedding the prompt and supporting re-audit.

recommended_forms.rationale: QI/audit project with measurement and change; QIAT is the specific assessment form.

draft_fields.qi_engagement: I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. I completed a baseline audit of 42 cases, introduced a triage prompt as an intervention, and completed a re-audit to measure change. I presented our findings and the improvement at the departmental governance meeting.
draft_fields.qi_understanding: By running this project, I developed my understanding of establishing baseline measures, designing and introducing a targeted clinical intervention, and conducting a loop-closing re-audit to demonstrate measurable improvement in clinical care.
draft_fields.reflection:
We completed a baseline audit of 42 cases evaluating time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. To address delays, we introduced a triage prompt intervention. The subsequent re-audit demonstrated an improvement in the time to antibiotics, reducing the time from 58 minutes down to 39 minutes.

Presenting these findings at the departmental governance meeting allowed me to share the positive impact of this simple intervention with the wider team. It highlighted how minor systemic adjustments at triage can expedite critical sepsis treatment.

This project demonstrated how targeted QI methodology can directly improve patient safety. Securing staff buy-in for the triage prompt was key to achieving this reduction in time to antibiotics.
draft_fields.next_pdp: Next year I will focus on embedding the triage prompt into routine practice and supporting a subsequent re-audit to the clinical improvements are sustained.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC1: be able to provide clinical leadership on effective Quality Improvement work (2025 Update)
SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I taught two F1 doctors and a medical student a structured approach to ECG interpretation using five anonymised ECGs from the teaching file. I collected verbal feedback afterwards.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee delivered an informal, small-group teaching session to junior colleagues using teaching file resources, which fits the TEACH form since it was delivered during routine work and did not involve formal assessor observation of teaching delivery.
If a senior colleague or educator was present to observe the trainee's teaching delivery and provide structured feedback on their teaching style and skills, this form would be appropriate.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I performed a shoulder reduction in ED with senior advice available but no formal DOPS. I documented neurovascular status before and after and reflected on analgesia planning.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee personally performed a shoulder reduction procedure without direct assessor observation, making this the correct form to log the clinical activity.
The trainee explicitly reflects on their analgesia planning and clinical decision-making during the case, which is best captured in a reflective practice log.

draft_fields.reflective_comments:
I performed a shoulder reduction in the emergency department. I senior advice was available if required, though this was not a formal DOPS assessment.

I documented the patient's neurovascular status both before and after performing the manipulation to safety and identify any immediate complications.

This case of analgesia planning prior to manipulation. I will I factor in adequate muscle relaxation and pain relief options early in my management plan for future joint reductions.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. On ACCS I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision. I checked contraindications, used sterile technique, obtained CSF samples and reflected on patient positioning.

recommended_forms.rationale: Trainee-performed procedure without a clear direct-assessment signal.

draft_fields.brief_description: I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision. I checked for contraindications before starting and used a strict sterile technique to obtain the CSF samples.
draft_fields.reflective_comments:
Performing this procedure of patient positioning in successfully obtaining CSF. The patient is optimally positioned before starting makes the landmarks much easier to identify and increases the likelihood of a successful first pass.

In future procedures, I will spend more time preparing and positioning the patient before opening the lumbar puncture kit, as this directly impacts the ease of the procedure.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways, then applied the learning by reviewing our local traffic-light guidance and changing my safety-netting.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways independently.
The trainee is reflecting on how they applied this self-directed learning to their local clinical practice and modified their safety-netting approach.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Self-directed learning: paediatric fever pathways and safety-netting
draft_fields.resource_details: Self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways, alongside a review of local traffic-light guidance.
draft_fields.reflection:
I completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways to refresh my knowledge of the risk stratification of febrile children. Following this, I reviewed our local ED traffic-light guidance to my clinical practice aligns with current departmental standards.

Applying this learning directly to my clinical work, I have adjusted how I approach safety-netting for parents and carers of febrile children. I now provide more structured, explicit instructions on what specific red flag symptoms should prompt a return to the department, clearer communication and safer discharge planning.

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I performed lung ultrasound on a breathless patient with suspected pulmonary oedema. I identified bilateral B-lines, discussed images with my consultant and reflected on integrating ultrasound with clinical assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: Visible source documents a procedure; log the procedure unless a formal observed assessment is confirmed.
Use if an assessor directly observed the documented procedure.

The trainee personally performed and interpreted a point-of-care lung ultrasound (POCUS) to evaluate a breathless patient, making this the most specific form for the technical and clinical integration of ultrasound findings.

draft_fields.case_reflection_title: Lung ultrasound in suspected acute pulmonary oedema
draft_fields.clinical_scenario: I assessed a breathless patient with suspected pulmonary oedema and performed a point-of-care lung ultrasound to aid my clinical assessment.
draft_fields.how_used: I performed a lung ultrasound to look for signs of interstitial syndrome, specifically assessing for the presence of bilateral B-lines.
draft_fields.usable_images: I was able to obtain usable images of the lung fields bilaterally.
draft_fields.interpret_images: I identified bilateral B-lines, which supported the clinical suspicion of pulmonary oedema.
draft_fields.learning_points: I discussed the images with my consultant and reflected on the integration of ultrasound findings with the overall clinical assessment to guide management decisions.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I reflected on a complaint about delayed communication with a family during a busy resus shift. The clinical care was appropriate, but I should have allocated a clearer update role and documented conversations better.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee is directly reflecting on a patient/family complaint about delayed communication, focusing on what happened, the learning points, and future practice changes.

As an ST5 trainee, managing and formally responding to complaints is a key management portfolio requirement. This entry can document the formal complaint investigation and resolution process from a management perspective.
The description is a personal reflection on communication, situational awareness, and resource allocation during a busy resus shift.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflection on complaint regarding communication during resus shift
draft_fields.key_features: A complaint was received regarding delayed communication with a patient's family during a highly active resus shift. While the clinical care provided was appropriate, the communication with the family was not managed effectively.
draft_fields.key_aspects: I provided appropriate clinical care to the patient during a busy shift in the resuscitation area. However, I did not allocate a clear role within the team for updating the family, and my documentation of our conversations was insufficient.
draft_fields.learning_points: I learned the importance of explicitly assigning a team member to update relatives during high-pressure resuscitation scenarios rather than assuming it will happen organically. I also recognised that clinical documentation must clearly capture the timing and content of any family updates to maintain an accurate record of communication.
draft_fields.further_action: I will that in future resuscitation scenarios, I designate a specific staff member to be the primary point of contact for the family. I will also be diligent in documenting all communication updates in the patient's medical records.
key_capabilities: SLO7 KC1: have expert communication skills to negotiate, manage complicated or evolving interactions (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC5: effectively lead and support resuscitation teams (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I contributed to a serious incident review after delayed recognition of sepsis in majors. I reviewed the timeline, identified handover gaps and learned to use an explicit trigger for senior review.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee actively contributed to a formal serious incident review, analyzing a case of delayed sepsis recognition, which fulfills a core Emergency Medicine management portfolio requirement.
The event directly involved reviewing a declared serious incident, focusing on the investigation, root cause analysis of handover gaps, and departmental learning.
The trainee reflects on personal clinical development, specifically identifying system-level handover gaps and establishing a personal cognitive trigger for future senior clinical reviews.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflection on Serious Incident: Delayed Sepsis Recognition
draft_fields.description:
I contributed to a serious incident review within the Emergency Department following a case involving the delayed recognition of sepsis in the majors area.

As part of this process, I reviewed the clinical timeline of the patient's presentation and journey through the department to understand where the delays occurred.
draft_fields.root_causes: The review identified critical handover gaps during shift changes which led to a loss of momentum in the patient's care and a failure to synthesise deteriorating vital signs.
draft_fields.contributing_factors: Contributing factors included high department volume and pressure in the majors area, which impacted the continuity of monitoring and delayed the escalation of the deteriorating patient.
draft_fields.learning_points:
I learned the importance of having an explicit, objective trigger for senior review when patients do not respond as expected or when vital signs trend downwards. 

I also learned how easily critical clinical information can be lost during unstructured handovers.
draft_fields.further_action: I will implement an explicit trigger for senior review in my daily practice for any patient with potential sepsis who is not improving. I will also my own handovers are structured and explicitly highlight patients at risk of deterioration.

draft_fields.project_description: I completed the Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) course today.
draft_fields.reflective_notes: The course refreshed my trauma team approach, the systematic primary survey, and effective communication with the trauma team leader. I reflected on the importance of structured communication during high-pressure trauma resuscitations and how a standardised approach minimises cognitive overload for the team.
draft_fields.lessons_learned: I learned to refine my structured handover technique in the resuscitation room and reinforced my systematic approach to the primary survey in major trauma.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. My consultant assessed me during a three-hour evening ED shift. I managed flow in majors, supported juniors, reviewed two unwell patients and reflected on maintaining situational awareness under pressure.

recommended_forms.rationale: Directly matches a formal, three-hour shift-level observation by a consultant, assessing non-technical skills (situational awareness, managing flow, supporting juniors) across multiple patients.
Applies because the trainee specifically reflects on their personal ability to maintain situational awareness and manage cognitive load under pressure during a busy shift.

draft_fields.reflection:
I managed flow in majors, supported juniors, and reviewed two unwell patients during a busy three-hour evening shift. I focused on maintaining situational awareness of the department's footprint while simultaneously providing clinical oversight to the junior tier.

This shift highlighted the challenge of balancing direct clinical reviews of unwell patients with the high-level oversight required to run the floor. To manage this, I I communicated clearly with the nurse in charge and the junior doctors, establishing clear triggers for when they should escalate concerns to me.

I will continue to practice structuring my floor management around regular brief updates from the nursing team and juniors. This helps prevent anchoring on single complex cases and departmental safety is optimised.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. Senior review prompted a PE workup. I learned to pause when the story does not fit and to document my differential more explicitly.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a patient with diagnostic complexity, initially anchoring on COPD before a senior-guided workup for PE, making it ideal for a retrospective discussion on clinical reasoning and differential diagnosis.
The trainee explicitly reflects on a diagnostic error (anchoring bias on COPD) and identifies key personal learning points regarding cognitive pausing and explicit documentation of differentials.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflecting on anchoring bias in a breathless patient
draft_fields.reflection:
I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. I focused on managing this as an acute exacerbation based on their past medical history and presenting complaints of shortness of breath. 

During a senior review, the consultant prompted me to consider alternative diagnoses and initiated a PE workup, which shifted our clinical direction.
draft_fields.replay_differently: I would perform a deliberate re-evaluation pause to challenge my working diagnosis before finalizing the treatment plan.
draft_fields.why: I allowed my initial clinical impression to be swayed by anchoring bias, focusing on the patient's known history of COPD rather than fully exploring other causes for their acute breathlessness.
draft_fields.different_outcome: The clinical outcome was safeguarded by the senior review, but a more structured diagnostic approach from the outset would have led to an earlier PE workup and reduced the delay in appropriate therapy.
draft_fields.focussing_on: I am focussing on incorporating a formal diagnostic pause into my assessment of patients with acute respiratory presentations, explicitly documenting a wider differential diagnosis list in the medical notes.
draft_fields.learned: I have learned to recognize how easily prior diagnostic labels can lead to cognitive anchoring, and the importance of actively looking for features that do not fit a suspected exacerbation.
key_capabilities: SLO2 KC2: aware of when it is appropriate to review patients remotely or directly and able to teach these principles to others (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. A consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries. They fed back that my explanations were clear but I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee delivered a structured clinical teaching session to an F2 doctor which was directly observed by a consultant specifically to provide feedback on their teaching skills.
To log the informal/bedside clinical teaching session delivered to the F2 doctor regarding ankle injury assessment.
To reflect on the consultant's feedback regarding checking the learner's baseline knowledge first and how to implement this in future teaching sessions.

draft_fields.reflection:
the hospital consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries in the department.

The consultant fed back that my explanations were clear and structured, but noted that I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first before diving into the teaching.

In future teaching sessions, I will start with a brief diagnostic question to gauge the learner's existing understanding. This will allow me to tailor my explanation to their specific level and make the teaching more efficient.

The activity happened yesterday in the Emergency Department. I was the trainee involved, the event was relevant to my training, and I reflected that I need to document the outcome and learning more clearly next time.

The trainee specifically reflects on their practice, noting they 'need to document the outcome and learning more clearly next time'.

draft_fields.reflection:
Yesterday, I engaged in rota management activities within the Emergency Department. As an ST5 trainee, managing the rota is a key component of my leadership development, requiring me to balance departmental staffing needs with trainee training requirements and contract compliance.

Upon reflecting on this activity, I realized that while the immediate staffing adjustments were resolved, I need to document the outcome and my learning more clearly next time. Having a structured log of these decisions helps maintain transparency and provides a clearer audit trail for rota changes.

In future, I will that any rota modifications, agreed swaps, or staffing escalations are formally documented immediately. This will help consolidate my management skills and clearer communication with both the management team and my clinical colleagues.

The activity happened yesterday in the Emergency Department. I was the trainee involved, the event was relevant to my training, and I reflected that I need to document the outcome and learning more clearly next time.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee is an ST5 registrar documenting their involvement with and entry into the departmental Risk Register, which is a core ST3-6 Management Portfolio requirement.

The trainee explicitly reflects on their performance ('I reflected that I need to document the outcome and learning more clearly next time'), making a reflective practice log highly appropriate to capture this personal learning.

draft_fields.reflection:
I participated in a risk management activity by identifying and preparing an entry for our departmental risk register. This allowed me to engage directly with clinical governance and the processes we use to mitigate systemic hazards in the Emergency Department.

While I successfully highlighted the hazard, I realised upon reflection that I must document the ultimate outcomes and systemic learning points more clearly. Simply raising the risk is not enough; tracking how the department responds and closes the loop is essential for genuine safety improvement.

In future risk register submissions, I will proactively follow up on the agreed actions and the resulting resolutions are clearly recorded in my own notes. This will help me better understand how clinical governance structures translate into active risk reduction on the shop floor.

The trainee presented the audit results and the proposed documentation prompt to departmental colleagues, delivering opportunistic clinical education.

draft_fields.reflection:
I completed an audit of documentation of capacity assessments in ED mental health presentations, reviewing 30 patient notes.

This audit highlighted inconsistent recording of capacity in our mental health presentations, which represents a clinical governance and legal risk. By presenting these results to the department and proposing a documentation prompt, I aim to standardize our approach and improve documentation compliance.

I learned how to systematically review clinical records to identify areas of vulnerability in our current practice. I will monitor the uptake of the proposed documentation prompt and plan to re-audit the notes to this intervention leads to sustained improvement.
